w:r.w:t: Comprehensive Resource for Students • Aligned with Curriculum Objectives
Social class, ambition, guilt, redemption, love, identity
Rural England (marshes, forge) and London, early 19th century
Born in Portsmouth, England; experienced childhood hardship when his father was imprisoned for debt
Worked in a factory at age 12, shaping his compassion for the poor and vulnerable
Used writing to critique child labor, harsh education, and social inequality
Published novels serially in newspapers, reaching readers of all classes
Legacy: Empathy, moral messaging, vivid portrayals of Victorian life
Elements of a Novel (Applied to Great Expectations)
Sequence of events: exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, resolution
Pip's journey from orphan to gentleman to moral awakening
Protagonists, antagonists, supporting characters who drive the story
Pip (protagonist), Magwitch (complex benefactor), Miss Havisham (tragic figure)
Time and place influencing mood and atmosphere
Social class vs. moral worth; ambition vs. integrity
Perspective from which story is told
First-person: Pip narrates his own growth and biases
Internal: Pip's shame vs. loyalty; External: class barriers, Magwitch's danger
Author's unique language and emotional feel
Rich description, irony, humor, pathos; tone shifts from innocent to disillusioned
PART ONE: A Boy from the Forge
Chapter Summary: Pip's Childhood & The Convict
(Philip Pirrip), an orphan, lives with his harsh sister Mrs. Joe and kind brother-in-law Joe Gargery, a blacksmith, in the marshes near the sea.
One evening in the churchyard, Pip encounters an
, who threatens him and demands a file and food.
Terrified but compassionate, Pip steals supplies from home and helps the convict.
Soldiers recapture Magwitch and another convict; Magwitch protects Pip by claiming he stole the food alone.
Pip feels guilt but never betrays Magwitch, valuing Joe's trust above all.
Pip is invited to play at
, a wealthy woman abandoned on her wedding day who wears a decaying wedding dress and has stopped all clocks at 20 minutes to 9.
, Miss Havisham's beautiful but cold ward, who mocks his "common" appearance and makes him ashamed of his background.
Pip falls in love with Estella and begins to desire becoming a gentleman to win her respect.
He visits regularly, pushing Miss Havisham in a wheelchair and playing cards with Estella, who alternates between cruelty and kindness.
(a kind village girl) that he wants to become a gentleman; Biddy wisely cautions him.
Chapter Summary: The Apprenticeship & Growing Restlessness
Miss Havisham arranges for Pip to be apprenticed to Joe; she gives him £25 as a "reward," ending his visits.
Pip signs apprenticeship papers but feels miserable—he no longer wants to be a blacksmith.
He fears Estella will one day see him dirty at the forge and mock him.
Years pass; Pip remains restless, dreaming of education and Estella.
Chapter Summary: The Great Expectations Revealed
arrives with shocking news: Pip has a secret benefactor and "great expectations"—he will inherit wealth, move to London, and be educated as a gentleman.
Pip assumes Miss Havisham is his benefactor and that Estella is meant for him.
Conditions: He must always be called "Pip," and the benefactor's name must remain secret until revealed later.
Joe supports Pip's freedom despite sadness; Pip buys new clothes and prepares to leave.
Chapter Summary: London Life & New Friendships
Pip arrives in London, overwhelmed by its crowded, dirty streets.
(the "pale young gentleman" he once fought at Satis House), who becomes his loyal friend and teaches him gentlemanly manners.
Pip meets other students, including the rude
, and is introduced to Jaggers' clerk,
Pip learns Miss Havisham's tragic story: abandoned by her fiancé Compeyson on her wedding day, she raised Estella to break men's hearts as revenge.
Pip secretly uses part of his allowance to help Herbert start a business.
At age 23, Pip still doesn't know his benefactor's identity.
(the convict) returns, revealing himself as Pip's true benefactor.
Magwitch made a fortune in Australia and returned to make Pip a gentleman out of gratitude.
Pip is horrified: his dreams of Estella and Miss Havisham were illusions; his wealth comes from a criminal.
He feels shame but also responsibility; he resolves to help Magwitch escape England, where he faces death if caught.
Chapter Summary: Confronting Truth & Planning Escape
Pip learns Magwitch's history: betrayed by Compeyson, transported to Australia, and driven by gratitude to help Pip.
Pip and Herbert plan to row Magwitch to a foreign steamship for escape.
Pip visits Miss Havisham, who regrets using Estella as an instrument of revenge; she promises to help Herbert financially.
Miss Havisham accidentally catches fire; Pip saves her, but she later dies, repentant.
Chapter Summary: The Failed Escape & Magwitch's Death
On the Thames, Pip, Herbert, and Startop row Magwitch toward the steamship.
Compeyson appears with authorities; a struggle ensues, both men fall into the river.
Magwitch is captured, badly injured; Compeyson drowns.
Pip stays by Magwitch's side in the infirmary; his distaste melts into compassion.
Pip tells Magwitch that Estella (his daughter) lives and is loved; Magwitch dies peacefully, knowing his "dear boy" never abandoned him.
Pip loses his wealth but regains his moral compass.
He returns home, reconciles with Joe and Biddy (now married with a son named Pip).
Years later, Pip meets Estella at the ruins of Satis House.
She has changed—softer, wiser, freed from Miss Havisham's influence.
Their final meeting is calm and forgiving; Pip sees "no shadow of another parting" between them.
Pip's shame about being "common" vs. Joe's integrity: "The only way to be truly uncommon was through honesty and goodness."
Pip's "great expectations" lead to pride and moral confusion; he learns "wealth and class" are hollow values.
Pip's guilt over helping Magwitch, lying to family, and neglecting Joe; he redeems himself by staying loyal to Magwitch.
Pip's love for Estella is based on illusion; true love requires seeing beyond appearances: "I have no heart" vs. Estella's eventual growth.
Pip's journey from orphan to gentleman to moral adult: "I am made by a gentleman" vs. "I was made by kindness."
Miss Havisham's revenge destroys lives; her repentance and Pip's forgiveness ("I forgive you") show healing is possible.
"My father's family name being Pirrip, and my given name Philip, my infant tongue could make of both names nothing longer or more explicit than Pip."
Creates intimacy; allows readers to experience Pip's growth, biases, and moral awakening
"The room was vast, filled with furniture, yet heavy curtains blocked out daylight... Everything that had once been white had turned yellow with age."
Establishes mood of mystery, decay, and psychological tension at Satis House
Marshes = moral ambiguity; broken handcuffs = broken social constraints; stopped clocks = frozen time/regret
Adds layers of meaning to setting and objects
Pip thinks Miss Havisham is his benefactor; reader suspects otherwise; Magwitch, the criminal, is the true benefactor
Builds dramatic tension; critiques social assumptions about worth
Joe (humble, kind) vs. Estella (proud, cold); Biddy (sensible) vs. Miss Havisham (delusional)
Highlights contrasting values and life choices
Magwitch's protection of Pip early on hints at later gratitude; Miss Havisham's fire foreshadows her tragic end
Q1: Does Pip feel nervous or excited about accompanying the soldiers on the convict search?

Pip feels nervous and fearful. He whispers to Joe, "I hope we don't find them," worried that Magwitch might see him and think he betrayed him. His anxiety shows his loyalty and moral conflict—he helped the convict but fears the consequences.
Q2: What surprising confession does Pip's convict make to the soldiers, and why do you think he did it?

Magwitch confesses: "I stole some food from the blacksmith's place over there... I'm sorry, but I ate your piece of meat." He does this to protect Pip from suspicion and punishment, showing unexpected gratitude and moral complexity despite being a criminal.
Q3: How does Estella treat Pip when they first meet, and in what ways does this encounter influence his self-image?

Estella treats Pip with disdain: "Look at his clothes—he's just a common working boy!" She mocks his hands and boots, making him feel ashamed of his background. This encounter plants the seed of Pip's desire to become a gentleman, driving his ambition but also his moral confusion.
Q4: What is unusual about Miss Havisham's appearance and lifestyle, and how do these reflect her past?

Miss Havisham wears a decaying wedding dress, has white hair, and sits in a dark room with all clocks stopped at 20 minutes to 9—the time she was abandoned. Her frozen, decayed state reflects her trapped psyche: she lives in the past, consumed by betrayal and revenge.
Q5: Does Pip tell the truth about his visit to Miss Havisham's house? Does Pip reveal the truth to someone later?

Initially, Pip lies to his family, making up a story "full of wealth, gold plates, and grand scenes that never happened." Later, guilt gnaws at him, and he confesses everything to Joe, who advises: "Lies are never justified... the only way to be truly uncommon was through honesty and goodness."
Q6: What happens when Pip meets the pale young gentleman, and what is unusual about the outcome?

The pale young gentleman (Herbert Pocket) challenges Pip to a fight. Pip lands a lucky punch, but instead of holding a grudge, Herbert smiles, shakes Pip's hand, and says, "You've won, fair and square." This unusual outcome—sportsmanship over anger—foreshadows their future friendship.
Q7: Is Pip happy about becoming a blacksmith after visiting Miss Havisham's house?

No. After meeting Estella, Pip feels ashamed of his "common" background and no longer wants to be a blacksmith. He dreams of becoming a gentleman to win Estella's respect, showing how external validation can distort self-worth.
Q8: What role does Miss Havisham play in Pip's apprenticeship, and how does this impact his expectations for the future?

Miss Havisham arranges for Pip to be apprenticed to Joe and gives him £25 as a "reward," ending his visits. This reinforces Pip's belief that she is shaping his future for Estella, fueling his "great expectations" and ambition—though these expectations are ultimately based on illusion.
Q9: How does Pip's relationship with Joe and Biddy begin to change as he becomes more focused on his new life in London?

Pip becomes increasingly proud and distant, forgetting those who truly care for him. He stops writing to Joe and Biddy, feels uneasy when Joe visits (calling him "sir"), and is ashamed to be seen with them. This shows how ambition and social climbing can erode genuine relationships.
Q10: Who unexpectedly visits Pip in London, and what shocking revelation does this person share?

Magwitch, the convict Pip helped as a child, unexpectedly visits. He reveals: "I'm the one who made you a gentleman." This shatters Pip's illusions about Miss Havisham and Estella, forcing him to confront the true source of his wealth and his own moral values.
Q11: Does Pip feel proud or ashamed when he learns that Magwitch is his benefactor?

Pip feels ashamed and horrified initially: "I wished he had never come." He is disgusted that his fortune comes from a criminal and that his dreams were based on false assumptions. However, over time, his shame transforms into compassion and loyalty.
Q12: How does Pip's relationship with Estella evolve by the end of the novel, and what lesson does he learn from their journey?

Initially, Pip loves Estella blindly, despite her cruelty. By the end, after suffering and growth, he meets her at the ruins of Satis House. She has changed—softer, wiser—and they part as friends. Pip learns that true love requires seeing beyond appearances and that personal growth matters more than social status.
Q13: How does Pip's character evolve from childhood to adulthood, and what key experiences shape his transformation?
Pip evolves from an innocent, compassionate child to an ambitious, prideful young man, then to a morally awakened adult. Key experiences: helping Magwitch (kindness), meeting
Estella (shame/ambition), receiving "great expectations" (illusion), learning Magwitch's truth (disillusionment), and staying loyal to Magwitch (redemption).
Q14: What does Joe Gargery represent in the story, and how does his relationship with Pip highlight themes of integrity and unconditional love?

Joe represents humility, kindness, and moral integrity. Despite Pip's pride and distance, Joe remains loyal and supportive: "Pip must go free... We've always been the best of friends." Their relationship highlights that true worth lies in character, not class, and that unconditional love endures despite mistakes.
1. Pip meets the convict in the village.
"I was just a small boy... in the churchyard"
2. The convict demands food and a file from Pip.
"Get me a file... And get me food"
3. Pip hides a piece of bread in his trousers.
"I hid the bread and butter in the leg of my trousers"
4. The guns firing signify a celebration.
"Another prisoner has escaped... they fired the guns as a warning"
5. Pip promises to bring the convict a file and food.
"Terrified, I promised to bring him a file and whatever food I could find"
6. The second convict's face is badly bruised.
7. Pip first meets Miss Havisham in the village.
"Uncle Pumblechook took me in his carriage to Satis House"
8. Estella treats Pip kindly during their first meeting.
"This boy? Look at his clothes—he's just a common working boy!"
9. Pip feels ashamed of his background after leaving Satis House.
"I thought about Estella—her scorn... and how she had made me ashamed of my clothes, my boots, and, most painfully, myself"
10. Pip lies to his family about his visit to Miss Havisham.
"I made up a story—one full of wealth, gold plates, and grand scenes that never happened"
11. Pip confesses the truth to Joe.
"I found Joe in the forge and confessed everything"
12. Joe advises that lies are never justified.
"He told me that lies are never justified"
13. Pip fights Estella at Miss Havisham's house.
He fights the pale young gentleman (Herbert)
14. The pale young gentleman shakes Pip's hand after the fight.
"Instead of holding a grudge, he shook my hand"
15. Pip is happy about becoming a blacksmith.
"I realized I no longer wanted to be a blacksmith—I wanted to be a gentleman"
16. Mr. Pumblechook informs Pip about convicts breaking into his home.
"Just then, Mr. Pumblechook came running toward me with alarming news"
17. Mr. Jaggers informs Pip about his "great expectations."
"Listen, then. I have this message for Pip. He has—great expectations!"
18. Pip expects to become a farmer.
"He is to come with me to London, where he will be educated as a gentleman"
19. Pip is supposed to go to London to become a gentleman.
"He is to come with me to London"
20. Joe is sad but supportive of Pip's news.
"No objection, Pip old chap," Joe answered sadly... "Pip must go free"
1. Where does Pip meet the convict?
(Text: "This gloomy, weed-filled place was a churchyard")
2. What does the convict demand from Pip?
C) A horse and a map
D) A knife and a blanket
3. What does Pip hide in his trousers?
4. What do the guns firing in the distance signify?
B) A warning about escaped convicts
5. What is Pip's reaction to the convict's threats?
A) He laughs at the convict
C) He promises to bring a file and food
D) He runs away without speaking
6. What does Pip notice about the second convict's face?
B) It is covered in dirt
D) It is pale and clean
7. Where does Pip first meet Miss Havisham?
8. How does Estella treat Pip during their first meeting?
9. What does Pip feel after leaving Satis House?
B) Ashamed of his common background
10. What does Pip lie about to his family regarding his visit to Miss Havisham's house?
A) His encounter with the convict
B) His visit to Miss Havisham
C) He made up a story
D) His fight with pale young gentleman
11. Who does Pip confess the truth to?
12. What does Joe advise Pip about lying?
B) Lies would not make him less common
13. Who does Pip fight at Miss Havisham's house?
14. What does the pale young gentleman do after the fight?
C) He challenges Pip to another fight
15. How does Pip feel about becoming a blacksmith?
16. Who informed Pip that convicts had broken into his home?
17. Who informs Pip about his "great expectations"?
18. What does Pip expect to become?
19. Where is Pip supposed to go to become a gentleman?
20. How does Joe react to Pip's news?
B) He is sad but supportive
B) Anxiety; fear about the future
26. What does "expectation" mean in the novel's title?
B) Hope or belief about the future
B) Training period learning a trade
B) Unable to be satisfied; greedy
B) The beliefs or opinions held about someone
31. Why does Pip feel ashamed of his background after meeting Estella?
B) Because Estella mocks his clothes and manners, making him internalize class prejudice
C) Because Miss Havisham refuses to help him
D) Because Biddy tells him he's not good enough
32. What does Miss Havisham's decaying wedding dress symbolize?
B) Time stopped by betrayal and bitterness
33. Why does Pip assume Miss Havisham is his benefactor?
C) She arranged his visits and seems to favor him
D) Estella promises to marry him
34. What is the significance of the title "Great Expectations"?
A) Pip expects to become a great blacksmith
B) The irony that social advancement doesn't guarantee happiness
C) Miss Havisham's expectations for Estella
35. How does Joe's character contrast with Pip's ambitions?
A) Joe encourages Pip's ambition for Estella
B) Joe embodies quiet integrity, contrasting Pip's restless ambition
C) Joe competes with Pip for social status
D) Joe manages Miss Havisham's household
36. The convict Magwitch's protection of Pip ("I stole some food") shows:
D) Desire to recruit Pip as an accomplice
37. What lesson about social class does the novel convey?
A) Wealth defines a person's worth
C) Kindness and integrity matter more than class status
D) Only education can change a person's class
38. Why does Pip stay loyal to Magwitch despite initial disgust?
B) Because he recognizes Magwitch's gratitude and his own moral responsibility
C) Because Jaggers orders him to
D) Because Estella asks him to
39. What does the final meeting between Pip and Estella suggest?
B) Personal growth and forgiveness are possible after suffering
C) Estella still looks down on Pip
D) Pip has forgotten his past
40. How does Dickens use first-person narration to develop theme?
A) It hides the truth from readers
B) It allows readers to experience Pip's biases, growth, and moral awakening firsthand
C) It makes the story less engaging
D) It focuses only on external events
"Though I was reluctant, I offered him mine"
"She tossed the cards down with a contemptuous smile"
"I felt a growing sense of apprehension"
Hope or belief about the future
"I was going to be apprenticed to Joe"
"My misfortune was that I believed illusions"
Ironic title; ambition vs. moral worth
Shows how external validation distorts self-worth
Core moral: integrity over social advancement
Theme of revenge and its destructive consequences
"I'm the one who made you a gentleman"
"We've always been the best of friends"
"I saw no shadow of another parting"
Hope for healed relationships after growth
Redeemed Man (stays loyal; reconciles with Joe; meets Estella as friend)
Always support answers with text evidence
, link character actions to larger ideas (e.g., Pip's shame → social class theme).
, eliminate obviously wrong answers first; watch for absolutes ("always," "never").
, track motivations and changes across the novel.
: Dickens critiques social class but affirms moral integrity—focus on this tension.
